blocks.body: Sentence Transformations — Modals shows how to rewrite meanings of ability, obligation, advice, and logical deduction through modal structures.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In modal transformations, you need to preserve not the word itself but the meaning: ability, necessity, advice, permission, prohibition, or deduction. That is what controls the rewrite.","rules":[{"label":"SAME MEANING","color":"emerald","text":"Pairs such as can -> be able to, must -> have to, and should -> ought to work only when the meaning and context still match.","example":"We have to leave now."},{"label":"CHECK THE NUANCE","color":"blue","text":"Some modal rewrites look similar but carry a different force: mustn't is not the same as don't have to.","example":"You mustn't park here."},{"label":"LOOK AT TIME","color":"amber","text":"In past contexts, modal meaning often moves into was able to, had to, should have, or can't have with a perfect infinitive.","example":"He must have forgotten."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. How to Spot the Needed Rewrite
blocks.body:
Before rewriting, ask what the original sentence actually expresses.

  • Ability -> is it a general ability, one successful past event, or a possible action?
  • Obligation / necessity -> is the rule external, or is the speaker drawing a strong conclusion?
  • Advice -> is it gentle advice, or criticism of a past action through should have?
  • Deduction -> is the conclusion about the present, or about a past event through must have / can't have?

blocks.heading: 2. Core Transformation Patterns
blocks.body:
Base sentenceTarget sentenceRule focus
I managed to solve the puzzle.I was able to solve the puzzle.managed to -> was able to for one successful result.
It is necessary for all visitors to wear badges.All visitors have to wear badges.it is necessary to -> have to.
It would be a good idea to leave earlier.We should leave earlier.good idea -> should as advice.
I'm sure he isn't at home.He can't be at home.certainty about impossibility -> can't for deduction.

Always check whether the rewritten sentence keeps the same force: obligation, prohibition, advice, and deduction are not automatically interchangeable.

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Confusing mustn't and don't have to.","wrong":"You don't have to smoke here. = Smoking is forbidden.","right":"You mustn't smoke here.","hint":"Mustn't = prohibition, don't have to = no obligation."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using can instead of the form for one successful past event.","wrong":"We could finish the report yesterday, and we did.","right":"We were able to finish the report yesterday.","hint":"For one real achievement in the past, was / were able to is often the better match."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Forgetting the perfect infinitive in deductions about the past.","wrong":"He can't forget the meeting yesterday.","right":"He can't have forgotten the meeting.","hint":"Past deduction often needs must have / can't have + V3."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"rose","title":"Making a modal swap without checking the strength of meaning.","wrong":"You must leave early. -> You should leave early.","right":"You have to leave early. / You should leave early. (only if the meaning is advice)","hint":"Not every similar-looking form keeps the same degree of obligation."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick Exam Check","items":["Name the meaning first: ability, obligation, advice, deduction, or prohibition.","Check whether the strength of meaning stayed the same after the rewrite.","Look at time: do you need a perfect infinitive or a form such as had to / was able to?","At the end, read the sentence as real communication, not just as grammar: does it still sound logical and natural?"]}
